text: During a totally laparoscopic distal gastrectomy with vagus nerve preservation, the surgeon spreads the greater omentum with assistance. A partial omentectomy is performed three centimeters from the gastroepiploic arcade. The surgeon identifies and dissects the layer between the omentum and mesocolon, clearing the greater curvature around the estimated gastric resection site. The left gastroepiploic vessels are ligated, without ligation from the root due to early gastric cancer.

The surgeon performs a right side omentectomy, dissecting the layer between the colon and the duodenum while carefully avoiding injury to the vessels supplying the colon and pancreas. Adhesiolysis is conducted if adhesions are present between the liver, gallbladder, and duodenum. The duodenum and pancreas head are identified to ensure precise dissection around the duodenum.

The surgeon identifies and preserves the anterior superior pancreatico-duodenal vein on the surface of the pancreas head. Dissection is performed around the right gastroepiploic vessels, with the right gastroepiploic vein being identified and ligated first. Further dissection reveals the right gastroepiploic artery originating from the gastroduodenal artery, with infrapyloric vessels located behind it requiring additional ligation. The greater curvature around the pylorus is cleared for gastric resection, followed by clearing of the lesser curvature above the pylorus. The right gastric artery from the proper hepatic artery is dissected and ligated. A linear stapler is inserted for distal gastric resection.

The first assistant provides vertical traction on the left gastric artery, facilitating the suprapancreatic lymph node dissection starting at the number eight lymph node area. The surgeon carefully dissects the eleven P lymph node area behind the pancreas, followed by further lymph node dissection around the celiac and splenic arteries, ensuring safety through meticulous layer-by-layer dissection.

The surgeon identifies the common hepatic artery and continues the dissection of the number eight lymph node. The portal vein is visible between the common hepatic artery and the gastroduodenal artery. Dissection is performed above the outermost layer of the artery to prevent vessel injury. The left gastric vein, also known as the coronary vein, is dissected and ligated. Careful dissection behind the left gastric artery reveals the celiac branch of the vagus nerve, visible as white fibers. The surgeon proceeds with dissection along the right cruce, ensuring the vagus nerve is not injured.

The surgeon performs lymph node dissection around the left gastric artery, taking care to avoid the vagus nerve. The lesser curvature near the esophagogastric junction is cleared. The anterior vagus nerve beyond the celiac branch is ligated, ensuring the celiac branch is not injured. The left gastric artery is ligated after identifying the celiac branch of the vagus nerve, and the clearing of the lesser curvature is continued.
The surgeon transects the stomach at the predetermined resection site, using a linear stapler to separate the gastric tissue.
